spec.emotional_state: frustrated about a small thing today
spec.label: C03_sporadic_d19

profile.personality: enthusiastic, makes more grammar slips, talks about football a lot

transcript.content: Hi Rohan, missed our chat! How was your exam prep last week?
It was okay, I think. I had a lot of work in the office, so I couldn’t study much. Also, my phone broke, so no practice online. Frustrating, na? I watched a football match though. Messi played again!
"Right, work and phone trouble make it tough. Watching Messi is a good way to unwind. What did you think of the match?"

Messi was on form, no doubt. But their team lost, which is sad. Anyway, I tried to read an English article yesterday to improve, but my concentration was bad. Work stress, you know? By the way, any tips for better English practice?
"Got it, work stress does mess with focus. Reading is a great habit. Try reading a bit every day, even 10 minutes. What kind of articles do you like?"

I like sports articles the most, like about football or cricket. Also, sometimes Bengali cinema news. But it’s hard to find simple English ones. Oh, and I tried to speak English with my friend yesterday, but I made so many mistakes. It feels frustrating. Any advice?
"Same, focused practice helps. Speaking with a friend is great, mistakes are part of learning. What part feels toughest, grammar, words, or confidence?"

Grammar and confidence, mostly. I know some rules, but when I speak, my brain freezes. Also, I mix up words a lot. Like, I say "do" instead of "does" or forget articles. It makes me feel shy to speak. And sometimes I can’t think of the right word for Bengali things—like "jatra" or "pulka." Do you have any tips?

"True, grammar can trip us in a hurry. Small fix: practice one rule at a time, like 'does' for third person. For Bengali words, try pairing them with English, 'jatra is a festival.' What word feels trickiest right now?"

Oh, the word "schedule" is giving me trouble. Like, how to say I have a busy schedule because of work and study. Also, when I watch a match, I want to describe the action, like "He scored a goal" or "The defender slipped." But I get confused with past tenses. Maybe I can practice that?
"Right, 'schedule' can trip us. Try: 'I have a busy schedule with work and study.' For past actions, keep it simple, 'He scored' or 'The defender slipped.' What match did you see?"

error_patterns.pattern: Uses present tense for completed past events in narrative context
examples: Last night I watch a film called *Koira Kahe Pabo*
They are best team in India

error_patterns.pattern: Uses simple present instead of present perfect for ongoing states since a past date
